title: Handling Errors
description: Learn how to handle network errors and bad HTTP responses gracefully.

blocks.content: Network requests can fail. The server might be down, the URL might be wrong, or the response might indicate an error. Writing code that handles these failures gracefully is essential for building reliable applications.

The `try / catch` statement lets you run code that might throw an error and respond to failures without crashing the program.

- Code inside the **`try`** block runs normally.
- If an error is thrown, execution jumps to the **`catch`** block, where you can log the problem or show a message to the user.

blocks.caption: Wrapping a fetch call in try/catch to handle network errors

blocks.content: As noted in the HTTP & Responses section, `fetch` only throws for network failures. A `404` or `500` response is still a successful Promise resolution — you must check `response.ok` yourself and decide what to do.

blocks.caption: Throwing a custom error when response.ok is false

blocks.title: Best practice
blocks.content: Always check response.ok inside a try/catch when writing production code. This handles both network failures and HTTP error codes in one place.
The playground below intentionally uses a broken URL. Run it to see the error being caught, then fix the URL to make it work.

blocks.title: Debug the error handler
Error Handling Quiz
blocks.description: Check your understanding of error handling with fetch.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the catch block in a try/catch statement?
options: To run code before the try block
To handle errors thrown inside the try block
questions.explanation: The catch block executes when an error is thrown inside the try block, allowing you to respond to failures gracefully.

questions.question: Does fetch() automatically throw an error when the server returns a 404?
options: Yes, fetch always throws on non-200 responses
No, fetch only throws on network-level failures
No, 404 responses are treated as successful
questions.explanation: fetch() only rejects (throws) when the network connection fails. 404 and 500 responses resolve normally — you must check response.ok.

questions.question: What does the following code log when the server returns a 403?
options: Failed to load data: HTTP error: 403
questions.explanation: response.ok is false for a 403, so the code throws a custom error. The catch block catches it and logs the message.
